trajectory_summary: Joe ran a clean, efficient call on a cooperative lead and landed a booked appointment. The core structural beats were hit in order, bill data was captured verbally without friction, and Joe's honesty on the payback question was received warmly. The call's primary vulnerability is a single protocol cave — the email-quote promise at 05:04 — which creates a handoff problem the closer must manage. Several verify items were skipped (co-owner, roof age, interest depth), and the bill was captured but never emotionally flipped.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a booked appointment at 4PM the following day, Corey's email confirmed, and explicit customer appreciation of the rep's honesty. Outcome should be tagged: appointment_booked. The appointment carries one structural risk (email-quote promise) but Corey's posture at close is cooperative and warm — this is a real sit, not a ghost risk.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. Of course. We'll send you an email as well.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This is the call's only critical failure by schema definition. Joe had the rebuttal available, Corey's request was a textbook trigger, and the cave creates a concrete handoff problem: the closer must now either send something Ion's policy prohibits or walk back a promise Joe made. On a cooperative lead like Corey, the rebuttal would have landed cleanly — there was no hostility to manage. Joe needs to hear the rebuttal, practice it, and understand that holding the line here protects the appointment rather than threatening it.

key_moments.quote: Is there a way to email me a quote?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's clearest protocol failure. The schema grades caving here as 'critical_failure' — and Joe's response ('Yeah. Of course. We'll send you an email as well.') is an unqualified cave with no rebuttal attempt. The closer will now walk into an appointment where Corey expects a pre-meeting email that Ion's policy prohibits sending. Reactive read holds: Joe had the rebuttal available and didn't use it.

key_moments.quote: That's a good question too. I'm I'm sorry. I'm not too sure on, like, the financial side of things. I just work in the scheduling department to get the design started.
key_moments.stewart_read: Joe's scope discipline here is correct and the honesty landed well — Corey responded 'Fair enough, man. I appreciate your honesty, mate.' The gap is not the disclaimer itself but the missing bridge. Joe stopped at 'I don't know' without routing the question forward: something like 'That's exactly what the specialist will walk through with you tomorrow — they'll show you the full payback timeline with your specific numbers.' The honesty built trust; the bridge would have converted that trust into appointment anticipation.

key_moments.quote: Very first. I'm getting another quote from another company on Sunday.
key_moments.stewart_read: Corey volunteers that he has a competing quote coming Sunday. Joe responds 'Awesome. Yeah. We recommend at least three.' — a generic affirmation that wastes a differentiation window. The schema's comparison_framing strategy ('We offer different equipment than most companies and our price points can be different') was available here and would have planted a seed before the Sunday appointment. Joe's response is reactive — he acknowledged the signal and moved on without using it.

key_moments.quote: Oh, I mean, I I pay a mortgage. So I think that's what I mean. I think Yeah. That counts.
key_moments.stewart_read: Joe asked whether Corey is on the title but never asked the follow-up required by the schema: 'Is anyone else on the title?' Corey's mortgage answer confirms his ownership but leaves co-owner status unknown. If a spouse or partner is on the title, the appointment is structurally vulnerable — the closer will arrive to find a decision-maker who wasn't included. This is a verify gap with a direct downstream risk.
